titleStmt.title: Letter from Adam Duffin to his daughter Dorothy Duffin, 7 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Adam Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter was sent by Adam Duffin (1841 – 1924) to his daughter, Dorothy Duffin (b. 1881).
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D2109/9/4/B DUNOWEN,
BELFAST. Sunday 7th Nov. 1915 Dearest Dorothy Your announcement of the
possibility, I may say probability
of your marrying a man, so far
totally unknown to us, while full of
hope for your future happiness, still,
you can understand, makes me a
little anxious and I am glad
that you have entered into no bonds
irrevocable on either side, until
you have time to consider, even if
not coolly, to consider the future &
to become pi more completely acquainted
I am sure you must have found
Mr Horsbrugh a clean, straight man
and a gentleman, or you would not
have listened to him, and so am
confident that it will be all right
but I do wish we could see and
know him and learn something
of his family, surroundings. I am
enclosing a note to him sowhich will
give him an opportunity of saying
something of himself & his position,
as I have no doubt he will wish
to do. I wonder if he is connected
with the Horsbrughs of Peebleshire, one
of whom married Sir Andrew Porter.
I was best man at his wedding, so many
long years ago. I need hardly tell you, dearest child
how very, very glad I shall be to
see you united to a man who really
loves and is worthy of your hand I
shall miss you terribly at home,
perhaps more than any of the others.
I have always fancied that you
had most apprehension of all the
anxieties I have tried to keep to
myself & had your silent sympathy. I am glad to tell you that they
weigh less heavily on me now than
they have done for years, & I am
very happy in thinking how splendidly
you have all come out & made us
proud of you.
Now, dear, you will be wise & I am
sure you know that your happiness
& such assurance of the future as
is possible is the one thought of
all of us at home. Your loving father Adam Duffin
